1: The address ʿabdî Yaʿaqōb ("my servant Jacob," H5650 + H3290) opens the section with a double covenant identity — Jacob the person and Israel the nation are one addressee. The parallel Yĕšurûn (H3484, "Jeshurun") appears only four times in the OT (Deuteronomy 32:15; Isaiah 33:5, 26; here) — it is a poetic, honorific name for Israel meaning "the Upright One," derived from yāšār (straight/upright). The tension is intentional: YHWH calls Israel by its ideal name even as they have been anything but upright. The MKT "Jeshurun" preserves the archaic honorific precisely because its contrast with Israel's actual history is theologically significant.
2: Yōtsĕrĕkā (H3335, "your maker/potter") from the root yātsar (to form, as a potter forms clay) is paired with ʿōśĕkā ("your maker," from ʿāśāh) — two different verbs for making, emphasizing both the creative forming and the purposive making. The phrase mibbeṭen (H990, "from the womb") grounds YHWH's involvement at the very origin of Israel's existence, before self-consciousness and choice. ʾal-tîrāʾ ("do not fear") is the covenant reassurance formula — the same formula used at theophanies (Genesis 15:1; Isaiah 26:24) now applied to the returning exiles. The MKT "who formed you from the womb: do not be afraid" keeps the participial construction to connect making with the command not to fear.
3: The Spirit-outpouring promise: ʾetsōq-rûaḥ (H3332 + H7307, "I will pour out [the] Spirit") uses yātsaq, the verb for pouring water or oil — a liquid image for divine Spirit. Zarʿekā (H2233, "your offspring/seed") and tsĕʾetsāʾêkā (H6631, "your descendants") carry both biological and covenantal freight: the Spirit is promised to the physical line that carries the covenant. This verse is the river behind the Pentecost: Acts 2:17-18 cites Joel 2:28-29 for the same Spirit-outpouring promise, and Isaiah 44:3 stands behind both — the "offspring" who receive the Spirit are ultimately fulfilled in the new covenant community of Pentecost. The MKT "I will pour out my Spirit on your offspring" preserves the liquid-pour image.
4: The simile: ḵĕʿāsāb (H6212, "like grass") and bên yĕʾōrîm ("beside streams of water") — the covenant community flourishing like water-fed vegetation after the desert of exile. Aravim (H6155, "willows/poplars") beside mĕyim (water) recalls the water-beside-willows imagery of Psalms 137:2 inverted: there, the harps were hung on the willows in silence; here, the people spring up beside the water-channels in abundance. The image answers exile with fertility — the same trees of mourning become the landscape of restoration. The MKT "they will spring up among the grass, like willows by flowing streams" captures the abundant, water-fed spontaneity.
5: The covenant belonging verbs: yiqrāʾ bĕšem YHWH (H7121, "will call on the name of the LORD"), yitkaneh bĕšem Yiśrāʾēl (H3655, "will take the name of Israel" or "surname himself Israel") — the post-exile return of Gentile adherents who will identify with Israel's God and Israel's name. The four phrases trace an escalating commitment: "I belong to the LORD" / "call by the name of Jacob" / "write on his hand 'To YHWH'" / "name himself Israel." 1 Peter 2:9 ("you are a chosen people... God's own possession") applies precisely this covenant-belonging language to the new covenant community. The "hand" inscription evokes the mezuzah-and-phylactery tradition but now written on the person, not the doorpost.
6: The theological summit of this unit: ʾānî rīʾšôn wĕʾānî ʾaḥărôn (H7223 + H314, "I am the First and I am the Last") — YHWH's claim to encompass all of time, from beginning to end. The construction is emphatic: both clauses begin with ʾānî (I), making the personal divine assertion unmistakable. This exact formula is taken up verbatim by the risen Christ in Revelation 1:17 ("I am the First and the Last"), Isaiah 2:8, and Isaiah 22:13 — a direct, deliberate transfer of YHWH's exclusive temporal identity to Jesus. Ûmibbaltî ʾên ʾĕlōhîm (H1107 + H410, "and besides me there is no God") closes the verse with absolute monotheism: the incomparability challenge of chapter 40 reaches its sharpest formulation. The MKT "I am the First and I am the Last; besides me there is no God" preserves the dual-pronoun emphasis of the Hebrew.
7: The courtroom challenge: mî-kāmônî yiqrāʾ ("who is like me, let him call out") — YHWH issues a challenge to any rival deity to demonstrate the same temporal comprehensiveness. Yagîdehā ("let him declare it") and yaʿarkehā ("let him set it in order for me") are legal-courtroom terms — the trial imagery begun in chapters 40-41 continues. ûmê-śūmî ʿam-ʿôlām ("and since I appointed the ancient people") — since YHWH established Israel as the covenant community, no rival deity has matched the historical claim. The question "who has announced from of old the things to come?" is the challenge no idol can answer: prediction + fulfillment is YHWH's unique credential.
8: ʾal-tirĕḥādû wĕʾal-tîrāʾû ("do not tremble and do not fear") — the double negative command repeats the covenant reassurance with the stronger verb rāḥad (to tremble/be terrified) before the familiar yārēʾ (fear). Hǎlōʾ mēʾāz hišmaʿtîkā ("Have I not told you from of old and declared it?") — YHWH points to his own prophetic track record as the evidence for faith. The climax: wĕʾattěm ʿēdāy ("you are my witnesses") — Israel is the witness stand that YHWH's self-revelation has historical validity. ʾên tsûr biltî ("there is no rock besides me") — the rock metaphor (H6697) recalls Deuteronomy 32:4, 31 (YHWH as Rock), the foundation metaphor that Paul applies to Christ in 1 Corinthians 10:4.
9: The idol polemic begins: yōtserê-pesel (H6459, "those who form a carved image") — pesel is the carved/hewn idol, from pāsal (to hew/carve). The verdict: kullām tōhû ("they are all worthless/chaos," H8414) — the same word used in Genesis 1:2 (tōhû wābōhû, "formless and void") and repeatedly in Deutero-Isaiah for worthlessness. The idol-makers' preferred things are worthless because ʿēdêhem ("their witnesses") neither see nor know (lōʾ yirʾû wĕlōʾ yēdāʿû) — the idols' inability to perceive is transferred back to their makers. The MKT "all who make idols are worthless" preserves the comprehensive kullām.
10: Mî-yātsar ʾēl ("who forms a god?") — the verb yātsar is now applied to idol-making: the same word used for YHWH "forming" Israel (v2) is used sarcastically for human "forming" of gods. Wĕfesēl nāsak (H5262, "pours out a metal image") — nāsak is the verb for casting metal, so the idol is both carved (pesel) and cast (nāsak). The question lĕbiltî hôʿîl ("for nothing useful") — the idol-maker's labor produces something that yields no benefit (yāʿal, H3276). The MKT "who forms a god, who pours out a metal image — for no useful purpose?" captures the rhetorical incredulity.
11: The craftsmen's solidarity: ḥăbērāyw (H2270, "his companions/associates") — the idol-makers form a guild, and they will all be shamed together (yēbōšû). Wĕhārāšîm hēmmāh meʾādām ("the craftsmen — they are only men") — the demythologizing move: behind the idol is not a divine craftsman but an ordinary human worker. Yitqabbětsû kullām yaʿămdû ("let them all assemble and take their stand") — court language again: even if every idol-maker stands together, they will be terrified and shamed. The MKT "let them all assemble and take their stand" preserves the legal-assembly sense of the challenge.
12: The blacksmith sequence: ḥārāš-barzel (H6213 + H1270, "iron craftsman") works with tongs (maʿătsād, H4621) and hammers (pattîš, H6360) in coals. The human vulnerability appears: wĕrāʿēb wĕʾên-kōaḥ ("he grows hungry and has no strength") — the god-maker gets tired and thirsty. The irony is stark: the deity he manufactures is immortal in the devotees' minds, but the craftsman who made it needs food and water to continue working. The MKT "he grows hungry and his strength fails, he drinks no water and is faint" captures the physical exhaustion that undercuts the pretension of the entire enterprise.
13: The carpenter sequence: ḥārāš-ʿētsîm (H6086, "carpenter/woodworker") stretches a measuring line (qāw, H6957), marks with a stylus (śered, H8279), shapes with planes (maqtsûʿôt, H4741), and outlines with a compass (mĕḥûgāh, H4230). The craftsman tools of precision — normally associated with skilled work — are applied to the making of what will become an idol. The result is kĕtōʾar ʾîš ("in the form of a human being") and kĕtipʾeret ʾādām ("like the beauty of a human") — the god is modeled on the human, not the reverse. This is the inverse of Genesis 1:26 (man made in God's image): the idol-maker makes god in man's image. Colossians 1:15 (Christ as "the image of the invisible God") is the answer to this perverse inversion.
14: The tree-selection process: the craftsman cuts down ʾerez (H730, cedar), takes tirzāh (perhaps cypress or holm oak, H8645), and ʾallôn (H437, oak) — trees that YHWH caused to grow over the years in the forest. The contrast is stark: YHWH is the one who planted the forests (v24, "I alone stretched out the heavens"); the craftsman harvests YHWH's creation to make a rival to YHWH. The wood that becomes the idol started as God's forest — it was raining on it, YHWH was sustaining it, and the craftsman then turns it into his "god." The MKT "he plants a cedar, and the rain nourishes it" preserves the divine-nurturance irony before the cutting.
15: The pivotal verse of the idol-polemic section: the wood is used for two purposes simultaneously. Yihyeh lĕʾādām lĕbāʿēr ("it becomes fuel for burning") — half burned for warmth (ḥammôtî, H2552, "I am warm"). And from the same tree: wĕʾap-ʿāśāh-ʾēl ("and he also makes a god") — the idol. The climax: yiśtaḥăweh lô ("he bows down to it"). The MKT "half of it he burns in the fire; over the half he eats meat" captures the mundane before the sacred act — the god is made from the same wood that cooked dinner. The theological point is devastating: the idol is a byproduct of fuel economy.
16: The detail: ḥeṣyô śārap bāʾēš ("half of it he burned in the fire") — on the literal half, he ʾāḵal bāśār ("ate meat") and was śābaʿ (satisfied/full). He warmed himself (ḥammōtî) and said: ʾāḥ ḥammōtî rāʾîtî ʾûr ("Ah, I am warm, I have seen the fire!"). The exclamation of ordinary warmth — "Ah, I see the fire!" — immediately precedes idol-worship in the next verse. The ordinary and the sacred are intertwined without any sense of incongruity in the craftsman's mind: that is the satire's point. The MKT "Ah, I am warm, I have seen the fire" preserves the artless exclamation that makes the subsequent worship so jarring.
17: The pivot: wĕšĕʾērîtô lĕʾēl ʿāśāh ("and the rest of it he makes into a god") — the residue of the wood becomes the deity. He yiśtaḥăweh lô ("bows down to it") and yitpallēl ʾēlāyw ("prays to it"). The prayer content: hatstsilēnî kî ʾēlî ʾāttāh ("save me, for you are my god!" H5337 + H410 + H859) — the most intimate covenant address (ʾēlî ʾāttāh, "my God, you are") directed at a piece of firewood. The irony is absolute: the saving-cry that belongs exclusively to YHWH is addressed to lumber. The MKT "save me, for you are my god!" preserves the direct address and urgency of the petition.
18: Lōʾ yādĕʿû wĕlōʾ yābînû ("they do not know and they do not understand") — the double epistemic failure that enables idol-worship. The reason: kî ṭāḥ mĕrĕʾôt ʿênêhem (H2902, "for their eyes are smeared/plastered over") — the verb ṭāḥ (to plaster/overlay) is used for coating walls; the eyes of the idol-worshiper are sealed with spiritual plaster, unable to see. Millĕhaskîl libbôtām ("their hearts from understanding") — the heart, the seat of discernment (śākal, H7919), is similarly sealed. 2 Corinthians 4:4: "the god of this age has blinded the minds of unbelievers, so that they cannot see the light of the gospel that displays the glory of Christ." The blindness of the idol-worshiper anticipates the blindness Paul describes.
19: The failed self-reflection: wĕlōʾ-yāšîb ʾel-libbô (H7725, "and no one calls it back to his heart/mind") — the idol-worshiper does not perform the reflective act of examining what he is doing. Dāʿat wĕlōʾ-tĕḇûnāh (H1847 + H8394, "neither knowledge nor discernment") — both cognitive capacities are absent. The quoted self-reflection that does not happen: ʾĕśāppenî tôʿabāh hûʾ (H8441, "surely it is an abomination!") — the word tôʿabāh is the strongest OT term for something utterly offensive to YHWH (used for the worst covenant violations). The idol-worshiper cannot bring himself to say what is obviously true: what he is doing is an abomination. The MKT "this thing in my hand is a detestable thing!" uses "detestable" for tôʿabāh to preserve the theological weight.
20: The verdict on the idol-worshiper: rōʿeh ʾēper (H7462 + H665, "feeds on ashes") — the shepherd-verb (rāʿāh) applied to ash-feeding; the one who should tend real flocks (like YHWH the shepherd) tends nothing but the residue of burned wood. Lēb hûttal (H5377, "a deceived heart") has led him astray — the heart that was not allowed to reflect (v19) has been deceived. Wĕlōʾ-yatssîl ʾet-napšô (H5337, "he cannot save his soul/himself") — the saving-cry of v17 ("save me!") goes unanswered because the idol cannot save. ʾAlōʾ šeqer bîmînî ("Is there not a lie in my right hand?") — the right hand that holds the idol holds a lie. The MKT "he feeds on ashes, a deluded heart has led him astray" captures both the feeding image and the heart-deception.
21: The turn to redemption: zĕḵor-ʾēlleh Yaʿaqōb wĕYiśrāʾēl ("Remember these things, O Jacob, O Israel") — after the idol polemic, YHWH commands Israel to remember (as opposed to the idol-worshiper who cannot remember, v19). ʿAbdî-ʾāttāh ("you are my servant") — the servant-identity counters the idol-worshiper's false servant relationship: Israel is servant to the living God. Yĕtsartîkā ʿebed-lî ʾāttāh ("I formed you, you are my servant") — the forming-language of v2 (yātsartîkā) recurs, grounding the servant relationship in the creative act. Yiśrāʾēl lōʾ-tiššāḵēḥ bî ("Israel, you will not be forgotten by me") — the covenant fidelity: YHWH does not forget, even if Israel has.
22: The forgiveness declaration: māḥîtî ḵeʿāb pĕšāʿekā (H4229 + H5645 + H6588, "I have swept away your transgressions like a cloud/thick mist") — māḥāh means to wipe away, blot out; ʿāb is a thick cloud or cloud mass. Ûḵeʿānān ḥattōʾtêkā ("and like a cloud your sins") — both pĕšāʿ (willful transgression) and ḥaṭṭāʾt (sin as failure/missing the mark) are swept away. The simile is perfect: morning mist dissolves completely and without trace — so the transgressions are gone. Šûḇāh ʾēlay kî gĕʾaltîkā ("Return to me, for I have redeemed you") — the call to return (šûḇ, H7725, repentance) is grounded in the already-accomplished redemption (gāʾal, H1350, to redeem as kinsman-redeemer). 2 Corinthians 5:19: "God was reconciling the world to himself in Christ, not counting people's sins against them." The cloud-swept forgiveness of Isaiah 44:22 is the same gospel Paul announces.
23: The creation doxology: rannû šāmayim ("sing for joy, O heavens") — sky, depths of the earth, mountains, forest, and every tree called to jubilate. This is the cosmic praise that follows the forgiveness of v22: the new exodus is a new creation event. The reason: kî-gāʾal YHWH Yaʿaqōb ("for the LORD has redeemed Jacob") and bĕYiśrāʾēl yitpāʾār ("and in Israel he will be glorified/beautified"). The verb pāʾar (H6286, to beautify/glorify) is YHWH being glorified in the act of Israel's redemption. Romans 8:22 places creation's groaning in the context of the redemption that will release it — the creation-rejoicing of Isaiah 44:23 is the answered groan of Romans 8.
24: YHWH's creation credentials: nōṭeh šāmayim lĕbaddî (H5186 + H905, "who stretches out the heavens alone/by myself") — lĕbaddî is emphatic: by myself, alone, without assistance. Rōqaʿ hāʾārets mî ʾittî ("who spread out the earth — who was with me?") — the rhetorical question expects the answer: no one. The Creator who acts alone is also the Redeemer who acts alone (v22). John 1:3: "Through him all things were made; without him nothing was made that has been made" — the alone-Creator of Isaiah 44:24 is the Logos through whom all creation came into being. The MKT "who alone stretched out the heavens, who spread out the earth by myself" maintains the double assertion of solitary action.
25: Mēpēr ʾōtôt baddîm (H6331 + H226 + H907, "who frustrates the omens/signs of liars/babblers") — baddîm refers to false prophets or soothsayers who speak empty things. Wĕqōsĕmîm yĕhōlēl (H7081 + H1984, "who makes diviners mad/foolish") — the Babylonian diviners, with their elaborate divination systems, are made into fools. Mēšîb ḥăḵāmîm ʾāḥôr ("who turns wise men back") — the wisdom of the empire is reversed by YHWH's action. 1 Corinthians 1:20: "Has not God made foolish the wisdom of the world?" Paul's critique of worldly wisdom stands in the same tradition: what looks like divine frustration of Babylon's wise men is a pattern that repeats in the cross.
26: Meqîm dĕbar ʿabdô (H6965, "who confirms the word of his servant") — the same verb (qûm) used in 46:10 for the divine plan that "stands": here it is applied to the prophetic word. The servant whose word YHWH confirms is the prophetic office (Isaiah himself), and the particular word being confirmed is the prediction of return and temple rebuilding. ʾāmar lîrûšālayim tûšāb ("says of Jerusalem, 'It shall be inhabited'") — the city that now lies in ruins is addressed by YHWH as the city that will be rebuilt. The prophetic word about restoration participates in the same "confirm/stand" language that applies to all of YHWH's counsel — his words do not return empty (Isaiah 55:11).
27: ʾĀmēr laṣṣûlāh ḥarābî ("who says to the deep, 'Dry up!'" H6723) — YHWH commands the deep (tsûlāh, H6683, the ocean abyss or deep waters) to dry up so that the rivers can be crossed. This is the new-exodus language: as YHWH dried the Red Sea (Exodus 14:21-22) and later the Jordan (Joshua 3:16-17), he will again dry the waters for the return from Babylon. Wĕnahărōtêkā ʾôḇîš ("and I will dry up your rivers") — the plural rivers may refer to the rivers of Babylon itself, or to the literal rivers crossed on the return journey. The MKT "who says to the deep, 'Dry up, I will make your rivers dry'" keeps the direct address to the waters.
28: ʾĀmēr lĕKōreš rōʿî (H3566 + H7462, "who says to Cyrus, 'He is my shepherd'") — the naming of Cyrus over 150 years before his reign is the chapter's most stunning historical prediction. But the theological weight falls on the word rōʿî ("my shepherd") — the same word as Psalms 23:1 ("The LORD is my shepherd," YHWH rōʿî). YHWH calls Cyrus his shepherd in precisely the sense that YHWH himself is shepherd: Cyrus will "tend" (accomplish the pastoral care of) YHWH's people by sending them home. Yiššālēm ḥeptsî (H7999, "will accomplish all my pleasure/purpose") — the divine purpose-language connects to the ʿēṣāh of Isaiah 46:10. The final phrase: yûssad hêḵāl ("let the temple be founded/laid") — the temple-founding is the specific commission of the shepherd-king. John 10:11 ("I am the good shepherd") takes up the shepherd-king role in its fullness: Cyrus is a type of the one who is both king and shepherd in the fullest sense, who gives his life for the sheep.
